findings.remediation: Freeze the dependency and verify the maintainer addition was announced upstream. Rotate any long-lived credentials the package ran against.

findings.signal: requests 2.32.0 was published at hour 03Z, outside the maintainer's historical window.
findings.baseline: Historical 90% window for requests: hours 14Z–16Z across 10 prior releases.
findings.remediation: Contact the maintainer out-of-band to confirm the release timing — legitimate maintainers rarely break their own circadian pattern without notice.

findings.signal: requests 2.32.0 was published to PyPI but no matching tag exists in https://github.com/psf/requests.
findings.baseline: Recent tags in source repo: v2.30.0, v2.31.0
findings.remediation: Publishing without tagging is the signature of a compromised registry credential. Pin away from this version, open an issue upstream, and preserve the sdist for incident response.

findings.signal: requests 2.32.0 modified its install-script content; the hash differs from the previous release.
findings.remediation: Review the diff of setup.py / __init__.py between the two sdists. Install-script additions are the signature of the event-stream and ctx-typosquat compromises.

findings.signal: requests 2.32.0 pulled in new dependencies: evilmod.
findings.remediation: A new transitive dependency is not automatically malicious, but verify the release notes explain it. Run this package through pipeline-check against the new dep's source tree.
Re-pin the dependency. Floating constraints let a compromised upstream release ship unreviewed into every fresh install.

findings.signal: Manifest packages 'requests' and 'reqeusts' differ by only 2 character(s) — possible typosquat.
findings.baseline: Typosquat campaigns publish names one or two edits away from popular packages (Levenshtein ≤ 2).
findings.remediation: Verify one of the two names isn't a transitive typosquat. Drop whichever was pulled in most recently until you confirm provenance.

findings.signal: A package named 'requests' was registered on npm 10 day(s) ago — matching a PyPI name in your manifest.
findings.baseline: Cross-ecosystem collisions registered within the last 30 days are a known dependency-confusion vector.
findings.remediation: Register the same name defensively on the other ecosystem, or add a resolver pin to prevent a package-manager lookup from silently switching ecosystems.
